name: pandas-indexing

metadata.name: pandas-indexing
metadata.summary: Helpers to facilitate working with pandas indices in particular multiindices

documentation:
# pandas-indexing helper

```{image} https://github.com/coroa/pandas-indexing/workflows/ci/badge.svg?branch=main
:alt: CI
:target: https://github.com/coroa/pandas-indexing/actions?workflow=ci
```

```{image} https://codecov.io/gh/coroa/pandas-indexing/branch/main/graph/badge.svg
:alt: Codecov
:target: https://codecov.io/gh/coroa/pandas-indexing
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/readthedocs/pandas-indexing/latest?label=Read%20the%20Docs
:alt: Read the Docs
:target: https://pandas-indexing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/pandas-indexing
:alt: PyPI
:target: https://pypi.org/project/pandas-indexing/
```

## Summary

`pandas-indexing` is a helpers package to make interacting with pandas multi-indices less
painful. It contains functions, that

- filter easily into multi indices: `isin`, `ismatch`
- add or update levels in a multiindex: `assignlevel`
- select one or multiple specific levels: `projectlevel`

## Usage

Given you have a time-series like dataframe with several multi index levels, like `model`, `scenario`, `variable`:
then you can select a subset with:

```python
df.loc[isin(model="m1", scenario=["s1", "s2"])]
```

or with shell like glob-patterns:

```python
df.loc[
    ismatch(
        model="REMIND*", variable="Emissions|**", unit=["Mt CO2/yr", "kt N2O/yr"]
    )
]
```

You can overwrite index levels:

```python
assignlevel(df, selected=1)
```

or project your data to only a few desired index levels:

```python
projectlevel(df, ["model", "scenario"])
```

All commands are described in detail in the API reference in [documentation] and are
togehter with introductions for installing and using this package, but they are mostly
bare-bones atm.

## Issues and Discussions

As usual for any GitHub-based project, raise an [issue] if you find any bug or
want to suggest an improvement, or open a discussion if you want to discuss.

[documentation]: https://pandas-indexing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/
[issue]: htts://github.com/coroa/pandas-indexing/issues/new
[latest branch]: https://github.com/coroa/pandas-indexing/tree/latest
[master branch]: https://github.com/coroa/pandas-indexing/tree/master
[pypi]: https://pypi.org
[readthedocs]: https://readthedocs.org/
[tox]: https://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

classes.name: pandas_indexing.utils.LazyLoader

methods.name: pandas_indexing.utils.LazyLoader._load
documentation: Load the module and insert it into the parent's globals.

Lazily import a module, mainly to avoid pulling in large dependencies.

`contrib`, and `ffmpeg` are examples of modules that are large and not always
needed, and this allows them to only be loaded when they are used.

Copied from tensorflow's agents.tf_agents.utils.lazy_loader by The TF-Agents Authors (2020), licensed under Apache 2.0
https://github.com/tensorflow/agents/blob/737d758452990dc3c81b8aeab1a6ae4f63afa12c/tf_agents/utils/lazy_loader.py#L28-L68

modules.name: pandas_indexing.selectors
documentation: Selectors improve `.loc[]` indexing for multi-index pandas data.

functions.name: pandas_indexing.selectors.isin
documentation:
Constructs a MultiIndex selector.

# Arguments

df

: Data on which to match, if missing an `Isin` object is returned

ignore_missing_levels

: If set, levels missing in data index will be ignored

filters

: Filter to apply on given levels (lists are `or` ed, levels are `and` ed)
  Callables are evaluated on the index level values.

# Returns

Isin or Series

# Example

```pycon
>>> df.loc[isin(region="World", gas=["CO2", "N2O"])]
```

or with explicit df to get a boolean mask

```pycon
>>> isin(df, region="World", gas=["CO2", "N2O"])
```

functions.name: pandas_indexing.selectors.ismatch
documentation:
Constructs an Index or MultiIndex selector based on pattern matching.

# Arguments

df

: Data on which to match, if missing an `Isin` object is returned.

singlefilter

: Filter to apply on a non-multiindex index (can also be handed into the `df`
  argument)

regex

: If set, filters are interpreted as plain regex strings, otherwise (by default) a
  glob-like syntax is used

ignore_missing_levels

: If set, levels missing in data index will be ignored

filters

: Filter to apply on given levels (lists are `or` ed, levels are `and` ed)

# Returns

Isin or Series

# Example

for a multiindex:

```pycon
>>> df.loc[ismatch(variable="Emissions|*|Fossil Fuel and Industry")]
```

for a single index:

```pycon
>>> df.loc[ismatch("*bla*")]
```

modules.name: pandas_indexing
documentation: Pandas indexing helper.

modules.name: pandas_indexing.units
documentation:
Unit handling in pandas data.

Enables unit conversions based on pint's application registry (see also Notes).

By default units are expected -- as in the IAMC default format -- on a `unit` level on
each row, but a column-wise `unit` level is also supported.

Units can be handled in one of two flavours:

1. {py:func}`convert_unit` converts manually to a new unit like `convert_unit(s, "km")`

2. {py:func}`quantify` convert data to a pint pandas array which tracks units implicitly
   through arithmetics until {py:func}`dequantify` then extracts the tracked unit back
   into the multiindex level.

   While this is in theory the simpler approach, the underlying library `pint-pandas` [^footnote-1]
   is brittle and breaks from time to time.

# Notes

The pint application registry is set by {py:func}`pint.set_application_registry` or
with {py:func}`set_openscm_registry_as_default`. The latter sets the IAMC based `openscm-units` one [^footnote-2].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import pandas_indexing as pi
>>> pi.set_openscm_registry_as_default()
>>> s = Series(
...     [7, 8],
...     MultiIndex.from_tuples([("foo", "mm"), ("bar", "m")], names=["var", "unit"]),
... )
>>> s = pi.convert_unit(s, "km")
>>> s
var  unit
bar  km      0.008000
foo  km      0.000007
dtype: float64
```

```pycon
>>> pi.quantify(s)
var
bar    0.008
foo    7e-06
dtype: pint[kilometer]
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: <https://github.com/hgrecco/pint-pandas>

[^footnote-2]: <https://github.com/openscm/openscm-units>

# See also

pint.set_application_registry

functions.name: pandas_indexing.units.quantify
documentation:
Convert columns in `data` to pint extension types to handle units.

[pint-pandas](https://github.com/grecco/pint-pandas) can only represent a single
unit per column and is somewhat brittle.

Parameters
---------- \{data}
unit : str, optional

> If given, assumes data is currently in this unit.

level

: Level of which to use the unit, by default "unit"

axis

: Axis from which to pop the `level`, by default 0

copy

: Whether data should be copied, by default False

# Returns

Data

: Data with internalized unit which stays with arithmetics

# Raises

ValueError

: If `level` contains more than one unit

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Series(
...     [7e-3, 8],
...     MultiIndex.from_tuples([("foo", "m"), ("bar", "m")], names=["var", "unit"]),
... )
>>> {example_call}
var
foo    7e-06
bar    0.008
dtype: pint[kilometer]
```

# Notes

pint-pandas uses the pint application registry, which can be set with
{py:func}`pint.set_application_registry` or
{py:func}`set_openscm_registry_as_default`.

# See also

set_openscm_registry_as_default
dequantify
convert_unit

functions.name: pandas_indexing.units.convert_unit
documentation:
Converts units in a dataframe or series.

Parameters
---------- \{data}
unit : str or dict or function from old to new unit

> Either a single target unit or a mapping from old unit to target unit
> (a unit missing from the mapping or with a return value of None is kept)

level

: Level name on `axis`
  If None, then `unit` needs to be a mapping like `{{from_unit: to_unit}}`

axis

: Axis of unit level

# Returns

Data

: DataFrame or Series with converted units

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Series(
...     [7, 8],
...     MultiIndex.from_tuples(
...         [("foo", "mm"), ("bar", "m")], names=["var", "unit"]
...     ),
... )
>>> {convert_unit_s_km}
var  unit
bar  km      0.008000
foo  km      0.000007
dtype: float64
```

```pycon
>>> {convert_unit_s_m_to_km}
var  unit
bar  km      0.008
foo  mm      7.000
dtype: float64
```

# Notes

Uses the pint application registry, which can be set with
{py:func}`pint.set_application_registry` or
{py:func}`set_openscm_registry_as_default`.

# See also

set_openscm_registry_as_default
quantify
dequantify

modules.name: pandas_indexing.datasets

functions.name: pandas_indexing.datasets.remindhighre_power
documentation:
Read IAMC power sector data from REMIND's HighRE IMP scenario for AR6.

# Returns

pd.DataFrame

: Time-series like power sector data

[^footnote-1]: Luderer, G., Madeddu, S., Merfort, L. et al. Impact of declining renewable
    energy costs on electrification in low-emission scenarios. Nat Energy 6, 32–42
    (2022). <https://doi.org/10.1038/s41560-021-00937-z>

modules.name: pandas_indexing.arithmetics
documentation:
Provide aligned basic arithmetic ops.

Simple arithmetic operations {py:func}`add`, {py:func}`divide`, {py:func}`multiply` and
{py:func}`subtract` which allow setting the standard how="outer" alignment that pandas
uses by default.

In practice, this means if dataframes do not share the same axes one can choose to get
the results for only the items index items existing in both indices (`how="inner"`) or
whether to prefer the axis from the first (`how="left"`) or the right (`how="right`)
operand.

# See also

pandas.DataFrame.align

modules.name: pandas_indexing.accessors
documentation:
Registers convenience accessors into the `pix` namespace of each pandas
object.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> df.pix.project(["model", "scenario"])
```

```pycon
>>> df.index.pix.assign(unit="Mt CO2")
```

```pycon
>>> df.pix.multiply(other, how="left")
```

classes.name: pandas_indexing.iamc.resolver.Context

Context shared between all Vars instances in a Resolver.

Notes
-----
Not to be instantiated by the user herself

classes.name: pandas_indexing.iamc.resolver.Var
documentation:
Instance for a single variant.

Attributes
----------
data : DataFrame
    Calculated data
provenance : str
    Formula for how the variant was calculated

Notes
-----
User does not interact with individual `Var` instances, instead she only ever holds
`Vars` instances.

classes.name: pandas_indexing.iamc.resolver.Vars

methods.name: pandas_indexing.iamc.resolver.Vars.antijoin
documentation:
Remove everything from self that is already in `other`

Parameters
----------
other : Vars
    Another set of derivations for the same variable

Returns
-------
Vars
    Subset of self that is not already provided by `other`

`Vars` holds several derivations of a variable from data in a `Resolver`

# Attributes

data

: Disjunct derivations of a single variable

context

: Shared context from the resolver

index : MultiIndex for which any derivation has data

# Notes

`Vars` are created with a Resolver and are to be composed with one another.

# Example

```pycon
>>> r = Resolver.from_data(co2emissions, level="sector")
>>> energy = r["Energy"] | (r["Energy|Supply"] + r["Energy|Demand"])
>>> r["Energy and Industrial Processes"] | (energy + r["Industrial Processes"])
```

classes.name: pandas_indexing.iamc.resolver.Resolver

class_variables.name: pandas_indexing.iamc.resolver.Resolver.context
documentation: context is shared with all Vars created

Resolver allows to consolidate variables by composing variants.

# Usage

```pycon
>>> co2emissions = ar6.loc[isin(gas="CO2")]
>>> r = Resolver.from_data(co2emissions, "sector", ["AFOLU", "Energy"])
>>> r["Energy"] |= r["Energy|Demand"] + r["Energy|Supply"]
>>> r["AFOLU"] |= r["AFOLU|Land"] + r["AFOLU|Agriculture"]
>>> r.as_df()
```

modules.name: pandas_indexing.core
documentation: Core module.

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.assignlevel
documentation:
Add or overwrite levels on a multiindex.

Parameters
----------    {df}
frame : Series or DataFrame, optional
    Additional labels
order : list of str, optional
    Level names in desired order or False, by default False
axis : {{0, 1, "index", "columns"}}, default 0
    Axis where to update multiindex
ignore_index : bool, optional
    If true, dataframes or series are not index aligned
**labels
    Labels for each new index level

Returns
-------
df
    Series or DataFrame with changed index or new MultiIndex

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.projectlevel
documentation:
Project multiindex to given *levels*.

Drops all levels except the ones explicitly mentioned from a given multiindex
or an axis of a series or a dataframe.

Parameters
----------    {index_or_data}
levels : sequence of str
    Names of levels to project on (to keep)
axis : {{0, 1, "index", "columns"}}, default 0
    Axis of DataFrame to project

Returns
-------
index_or_data : Index|MultiIndex|Series|DataFrame

See also
--------
pandas.MultiIndex.droplevel
pandas.Series.droplevel
pandas.DataFrame.droplevel

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.concat
documentation:
Concatenate pandas objects along a particular axis.

In addition to the functionality provided by pd.concat, if the concat axis has a multiindex
then the level order is reordered consistently.

Parameters
----------
objs : a sequence or mapping of Series, DataFrame or Index objects
    If a mapping is passed the keys will be used as a new index level
    (with the name of the `keys` argument).
order : a sequence of str, default None
    The order of level names in which to concatenate
axis : Axis
    Axis along which to concatenate
keys : str or list-like of str
    If `objs` is a mapping, a string-like value will be used as name of the new level,
    otherwise it is passed on to pd.concat.
copy : bool, default False
    Whether to copy the underlying data
**concat_kwds
    Other arguments accepted by pd.concat

Returns
-------
Concatenated data or index

Raises
------
ValueError
    If the level names of `objs` do not match

See also
--------
pandas.concat

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.dropnalevel
documentation:
Remove missing index values.

Drops all index entries for which any or all (`how`) levels are
undefined.

Parameters
---------- \{index_or_data}
subset : Sequence[str], optional

> Names of levels on which to check for NA values

how

: Whether to remove an entry if all levels are NA or only a single one

axis

: Axis of DataFrame to check on

# Returns

index_or_data : Index|MultiIndex|Series|DataFrame

# See also

pandas.DataFrame.dropna
pandas.Series.dropna
pandas.Index.dropna

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.uniquelevel
documentation:
Return unique index levels.

Parameters
----------    {index_or_data}
levels : str or Sequence[str], optional
    Names of levels to get unique values of
axis : {{0, 1, "index", "columns"}}, default 0
    Axis of DataFrame to check on

Returns
-------
unique_index : Index

See also
--------
pandas.Index.unique

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.describelevel
documentation:
Describe index levels.

Parameters
----------    {index_or_data}
n : int, default 80
    The maximum line length
as_str : bool, default False
    Whether to return as string or print, instead

Returns
-------
description : str, optional
    if as_str is True

See also
--------
pandas.DataFrame.describe

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.semijoin
documentation:
Semijoin *frame_or_series* by index *other*.

Parameters
----------    {frame_or_series}
other : Index
    Other index to join with
how : {{'left', 'right', 'inner', 'outer'}}
    Join method to use
level : None or str or int or
    Single level on which to join, if not given join on all
sort : bool, optional
    Whether to sort the index
axis : {{0, 1, "index", "columns"}}
    Axis on which to join
fill_value
    Value for filling gaps introduced by right or outer joins
fail_on_reorder : bool, default False
    Raise ValueError if index order cannot be guaranteed

Returns
-------
DataFrame or Series

Raises
------
ValueError
    If *fail_on_reorder* is True and the new index order does not correspond
    to the order of other
ValueError
    If *axis* is not 0, "index" or 1, "columns"
TypeError
    if *frame_or_series* does not derive from DataFrame or Series

See also
--------
pandas.Index.join

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.antijoin
documentation:
Antijoin *index_or_data* with index *other*.

ie remove all occurrences of other from data

Parameters
----------    {index_or_data}
other : Index
    Other index to join with
level : None or str or int or
    Single level on which to join, if not given join on all
axis : {{0, 1, "index", "columns"}}
    Axis on which to join

Returns
-------
Index or DataFrame or Series

Raises
------
ValueError
    If axis is not 0, "index" or 1, "columns"
TypeError
    if index_or_data does not derive from DataFrame or Series

See also
--------
pandas.Index.join

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.extractlevel
documentation:
Extract new index levels with *templates* matched against any index
level.

The `**templates` argument defines pairs of level names and templates. Given level
names are matched against the template, f.ex. `"Emi|{{gas}}|{{sector}}"`. Patterns
(`{{gas}}` or `{{sector}}`) appearing in the template are extracted from the
successful matches and added as new levels.

Pattern names in the `optional` argument can be missing (including a leading `|`
character) and are replaced by the string `"Total"` then.

:::{versionchanged} 0.5.3 Added optional patterns.
:::

:::{versionchanged} 0.5.0 *drop* replaced by *keep* and default changed to not keep. *regex* added.
:::

Parameters
---------- \{index_or_data} template : str, optional

> Extraction template for a single level

keep

: Whether to keep the split dimension

dropna

: Whether to drop the non-matching levels

regex

: Whether templates are given as regular expressions (regexes must use named
  captures)

axis

: Axis of DataFrame to extract from

drop

: Deprecated argument, use keep instead

optional

: Marks templates as optional

templates

: Templates for splitting one or multiple levels

# Returns

Index, Series or DataFrame

# Raises

ValueError

: If `dim` is not a dimension of `index_or_series`

ValueError

: If `template` is given, while index has more than one level

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Series(
...     range(4),
...     MultiIndex.from_arrays(
...         [
...             ["SE|Elec|Bio", "SE|Elec|Coal", "PE|Coal", "SE|Elec"],
...             ["GWh", "GWh", "EJ", "GWh"],
...         ],
...         names=["variable", "unit"],
...     ),
... )
>>> s
variable      unit
SE|Elec|Bio   GWh     0
SE|Elec|Coal  GWh     1
PE|Coal       EJ      2
SE|Elec       GWh     3
dtype: int64
>>> extractlevel(s, variable="SE|{{type}}|{{fuel}}", keep=True)
variable      unit  type  fuel
SE|Elec|Bio   GWh   Elec  Bio     0
SE|Elec|Coal  GWh   Elec  Coal    1
dtype: int64
```

```pycon
>>> extractlevel(s, variable="SE|{{type}}|{{fuel}}")
unit  type  fuel
GWh   Elec  Bio     0
GWh   Elec  Coal    1
dtype: int64
```

```pycon
>>> extractlevel(s, variable="SE|{{type}}|{{fuel}}", optional=["fuel"])
unit  type  fuel
GWh   Elec  Bio     0
GWh   Elec  Coal    1
GWh   Elec  Total   3
dtype: int64
```

```pycon
>>> extractlevel(s, variable="SE|{{type}}|{{fuel}}", keep=True, dropna=False)
variable      unit  type  fuel
SE|Elec|Bio   GWh   Elec  Bio     0
SE|Elec|Coal  GWh   Elec  Coal    1
PE|Coal       EJ    NaN   NaN     2
SE|Elec       GWh   NaN   NaN     3
dtype: int64
```

```pycon
>>> extractlevel(s, variable=r"SE\|(?P<type>.*?)(?:\|(?P<fuel>.*?))?", regex=True)
unit  type  fuel
GWh   Elec  Bio     0
GWh   Elec  Coal    1
GWh   Elec  NaN     3
dtype: int64
```

```pycon
>>> s = Series(range(3), ["SE|Elec|Bio", "SE|Elec|Coal", "PE|Coal"])
>>> extractlevel(s, "SE|{{type}}|{{fuel}}")
type  fuel
Elec  Bio     0
      Coal    1
dtype: int64
```

# See also

formatlevel

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.formatlevel
documentation:
Format index levels based on a *template* which can refer to other
levels.

:::{versionchanged} 0.5.3 Added optional patterns.
:::

Parameters
---------- \{index_or_data}
drop : bool, default False

> Whether to drop the used index levels

axis

: Axis of DataFrame to modify

optional

: Marks levels as optional (including a leading | character)

templates

: Format templates for one or multiple levels

# Returns

Index, Series or DataFrame

# Raises

ValueError

: If *templates* refer to non-existant levels

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.fixindexna
documentation:
Fix broken MultiIndex NA representation from .groupby(..., dropna=False)

Refer to https://github.com/coroa/pandas-indexing/issues/25 for details

Parameters
----------    {index_or_data}
axis : Axis, optional
    Axis to fix, by default 0

Returns
-------
index_or_data

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.to_tidy
documentation:
Convert multi-indexed time-series dataframe to tidy dataframe.

Parameters
---------- \{data}
meta : DataFrame, optional

> Meta data that is joined before tidying up

value_name

: Column name for the values; default "value"
  Use `None` to not change the name.

columns

: Name for the level on the columns axis; default "year"
  Use `None` to not change the name.

# Returns

DataFrame

: Tidy dataframe without index

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.aggregatelevel
documentation:
Aggregate labels on one or multiple levels together.

Parameters
---------- \{data}
agg_func : str, optional

> Function for aggregating values, default "sum"
> Other sensible options are "mean" or "first"

axis

: Axis on which to aggregate, default 0

dropna

: Whether to drop or preserve NANs in the index, default True

mode

: Whether to replace or to append to the individual labels or return
  the aggregated data

levels

: Mapping for one or multiple levels, which labels to aggregate under a
  common name f.ex. `region={{"sdn_ssd": ["sdn", "ssd"]}}` aggregates
  the "sdn" and "ssd" regions to a new "sdn_ssd" region.

# Returns

Data

: Aggregated data

# Notes

If you already have a complete mapping from country to region, then prefer
to use groupby directly instead of relying on this relatively slow method.

# See also

pandas.DataFrame.groupby

functions.name: pandas_indexing.core.add_zeros_like
documentation:
Add explicit *levels* to *data* as 0 values.

Remaining levels in *data* not found in *levels* or *derive* are taken from
*reference* (or its index).

Parameters
----------    {data}
reference : Index
    expected level labels (like model, scenario combinations)
derive : dict
    derive labels in a level from a multiindex with allowed combinations
**levels : [str]
    which labels should be added to df

Returns
-------
DataFrame
    unsorted data with additional zero data
